title: Reading brood disease symptoms on the frame
subtitle: Distinguishing chalk brood, sacbrood, and the notifiable foulbroods.
excerpt: Unhealthy brood has a look and, sometimes, a smell. Being able to tell chalk brood from sacbrood from European foulbrood from American foulbrood is the first skill of brood-disease management. Here's what to look for on each frame and what to do next.
sourceNotes: NBU Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024), brood-disease identification section. NBU BeeBase disease guide. BBKA exam syllabus material on AFB and EFB. Bee Craft seasonal notes on chalk brood.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fungal disease (Ascosphaera apis) that turns bee larvae into hard, white or grey chalk-like mummies in the cells.
A notifiable bacterial disease (Paenibacillus larvae) that kills sealed brood, leaving a sunken, perforated cappings and a ropy brown mass in the cell.
A bacterial disease (Melissococcus plutonius) that kills unsealed larvae, which turn brown and twisted in the cell.

content.text: Healthy brood looks the same way every time: white larvae curled at the base of their cells, sealed with flat pale-brown cappings in a tight regular pattern. Anything that looks different is worth investigating. Most brood problems are non-notifiable and manageable. Two are notifiable in Great Britain: American foulbrood and European foulbrood. You must contact your regional bee inspector if you find either.
Look for: hard, chalky white or grey-black pellets in cells or on the landing board. The pellets are individual mummified larvae, each the length of a bee larva. Chalk brood, caused by the fungus

(Ascosphaera apis), is most common in damp spring weather and in shaded, poorly ventilated hives. A light infection clears itself when the colony strengthens and the weather improves. A heavy infection that persists through summer warrants requeening with stock from a less susceptible strain.

Look for: uncapped or sunken-capped cells with a larva that has turned from white to yellow to brown. Lift the larva out with a matchstick or thin wire, it comes out as a sac of liquid, the skin intact but the contents fluid. Sacbrood is a viral condition, not bacterial or fungal, and is not notifiable. It usually clears by mid-summer as the colony builds up worker numbers. No treatment exists; good colony hygiene and re-queening with a hygienic strain helps.
Look for: unsealed larvae twisted in the cell, often lying across the cell wall rather than curled at the base. Colour ranges from creamy-yellow to brown. There may be a sour smell.
is notifiable in GB. Contact your regional bee inspector at NBU BeeBase immediately, do not move the frames.

Look for: sealed brood with sunken, greasy-looking, sometimes perforated cappings. The cells beneath smell strongly of rotting animal. The ropy test: insert a matchstick into the cell and withdraw it slowly, if the brown sticky mass inside stretches into a thread 2 cm or longer before snapping, this is very likely
. It is notifiable. Contact your regional bee inspector at NBU BeeBase immediately. Do not move the hive, do not swap equipment between hives.

European foulbrood and American foulbrood are both notifiable under the Bee Diseases and Pests Control Order 2006. Report a suspected case via NBU BeeBase (nationalbeeunit.com) or by phone to your regional bee inspector. Inspection is free. If you destroy a colony before the inspector visits, you may lose any compensation you're entitled to and make diagnosis impossible.
